behaviors.description: The generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) specified in the description
behaviors.justification: The core failure is whether the real LLM generates code with the correct signature. A pure mock that always returns correct signatures would hide real LLM failures. The service must handle actual LLM output variability. While unit tests could verify signature parsing logic with mocked code, catching the real failure (LLM generates wrong signature) requires calling the real LLM.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real code generation service with a function description specifying parameter names, types, and return type. Extract the generated code's function signature via AST parsing or regex. Verify the signature matches the spec exactly (parameter count, names, type annotations, return type). Run multiple times to account for LLM non-determinism. Fail if any generation produces incorrect signature.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test description requires real LLM call (signature correctness depends on LLM behavior, not just service parsing logic).

behaviors.description: The generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt
behaviors.justification: The success of generated code passing tests depends entirely on what the real LLM produces. Different LLM calls will generate different implementations—some may pass tests, some may fail. Mocking the LLM to always return correct code would hide real failures where the LLM generates incorrect logic. Only real LLM calls can verify whether the service successfully generates executable, test-passing code.
behaviors.test_description: For a function with provided unit tests and a description that represents a non-trivial implementation task, call the service and execute the generated code against all test cases. Verify that generated code either passes all tests on first attempt or eventually passes after refinement loops (up to 3 attempts). Track pass/fail status for each execution. Repeat with multiple function prompts to capture LLM behavior variation.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real LLM because correctness depends on generated code quality, which varies with real model calls.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from its mistakes
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests the service's prompt-construction logic: does it properly capture test failure messages and include them in the next LLM call? This can be verified by mocking the LLM to return code that fails known test cases, then inspecting the subsequent prompt to confirm failure messages are present. The service logic is deterministic—no real LLM is needed. Unit tests could verify isolated prompt-building, but integration tests are minimally necessary to verify the full capture→refine→prompt flow.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return code that fails specific test cases (with known failure messages). Execute tests against generated code and capture failure output. Intercept or inspect the next prompt sent to the (mocked) LLM. Verify that the prompt text includes the test failure messages, error stack traces, or assertion failures. Use string matching or structured prompt parsing. Do not require the real LLM to actually improve; only verify the service constructs correct prompts.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test can use mocked LLM since it verifies service logic (prompt construction), not LLM learning. Mocking still provides realistic failure messages.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error with all three attempts and their test results
behaviors.justification: This tests service control flow and response structure: attempt counting, exit condition, and error serialization. All deterministic. Can mock the LLM to always return failing code, triggering the exit path reliably. No real LLM is needed to verify that the service counts to 3, stops, and constructs the error response correctly. The test only verifies the service's state machine and response format.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM client to always return code that fails all provided tests (e.g., syntax error, logic error causing test failures). Call the service and expect it to attempt generation up to 3 times, then return a structured error response. Verify: (1) exactly 3 generation attempts were made, (2) each attempt's test results are included in the error, (3) the response includes all attempts' generated code and failure reasons, (4) no further attempts occur after the 3rd failure. Assert on response schema and content.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test uses mocked LLM to trigger failure path predictably. No real LLM needed since this is service state-machine logic.

behaviors.description: The full loop — generate, test, refine, test again — produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong
behaviors.justification: This tests the entire agentic pipeline: multiple LLM calls (initial generation + refinement attempts) orchestrated by the service, where each call's output feeds into service logic that determines the next prompt. The failure mode is 'the service cannot recover from a bad initial generation via iterative refinement.' Mocking the LLM to gradually improve on each iteration would not test the real behavior—only real LLM calls can show whether refinement prompts actually cause improvement. This is a multi-step agentic workflow, not a single LLM call.
behaviors.test_description: Select a function description known to be non-trivial (one that a single LLM call usually fails on). Call the service and allow it to run the full refinement loop (up to 3 attempts). For each iteration: (1) inspect the generated code and test results, (2) if tests fail, verify the service updated the prompt with failures, (3) continue to next iteration. Assert that generated code eventually passes all tests OR the service terminates with all 3 attempts documented. Run on multiple real LLM prompts to capture realistic success/failure patterns. Verify the loop terminates correctly and final result is correct or error is properly structured.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires real LLM because the test is about whether iterative refinement actually recovers from initial failure—mocking would not expose real LLM behavior. Multiple agentic steps (generate, refine) with service logic between them constitutes a workflow.
